fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BOYLE J:

These are my ex tempore reasons taken from the transcript, corrected for grammatical errors and to make my oral reasons more readable.
The father seeks orders that X aged five and Y aged two (“the children”) live with him, and he have sole decision-making responsibility for them. The mother currently spends supervised time with the children, pursuant to orders. The father seeks supervision continue until Y starts school in 2028, and provided the mother fulfils a number of conditions.
The matter was heard on an undefended basis. The mother did not comply with directions for filing made by the Court on 21 October 2025, nor 2 December 2025. The orders of 2 December 2025 extended the dates for her to file and serve an Amended Response and affidavit to 9 December 2025. The orders made clear the matter would proceed undefended if the mother did not file her material in accordance with the orders, and that is the basis upon which the matter has proceeded. The mother attended at court and made submissions.
The Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) largely supported the orders sought by the father, contained in a Minute of Orders provided on the first day of the hearing. The orders are similar to those previously sought by the father. The ICL did not support an order for the mother to be solely responsible for the costs of supervision of her time with the children.
Where the father sought the mother not permit any time at all to occur between the children and her brother Mr G, the ICL initially sought the mother be present for any time between her brother and the children. That position changed during the course of the day, and following the tender of Mr G’s criminal record. By the conclusion of the hearing, neither the ICL, the father nor the mother supported an order for time between Mr G and the children.
The father and the ICL both relied on Case Outline documents which set out the material they relied on. I have had regard to that material, along with the various exhibits tendered during the course of the hearing.
Although the proceedings are undefended, I must follow the statutory pathway and satisfy myself that the orders I make are in the best interests of the children, independent of any agreement between the father and ICL. Section 60CA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) provides the court must regard the best interests of the children as the paramount consideration in making a parenting order.
Section 60CC of the Act sets out relevantly the list of matters to be considered in determining what is in a child’s best interests. That is not an exhaustive list. Relevant matters will be referred to during the course of these Reasons, in particular the benefit to the children having a relationship with their parents where it is safe for them to do so and the capacity of both parents to meet the children’s needs. This issue of safety is one that has been at the forefront of the hearing and so it is within these Reasons.

BACKGROUND

The father is aged 42 years and the mother 27 years. They commenced a relationship in 2019. They met when the father engaged the mother’s services as a sex worker.
The mother has been diagnosed and treated for ADHD. She has been prescribed amphetamine style medication to treat her condition.
The parties separated on 5 December 2023.
An apprehended violence order (“AVO”) was applied for by NSW Police for the protection of the father and the children from the mother after an incident at the home in late 2023. The children remained living with the mother and spent time with the father. Arrangements for the children differed as Y was then an infant and being breastfed by her mother. The father spent overnight time with X and daytimes with Y around the changeovers for X, as I understand it.
The children moved into the father’s care following an interim hearing on 3 December 2024, and orders were put in place for supervised time.

ISSUES

(a) Is it in the best interests of the children for time with their mother to be supervised until Y starts school?

(b) How should the costs of supervision be met?

(c) Should the Court impose drug testing and ongoing psychiatric involvement or engagement for the mother for time to move to unsupervised time?

There is no issue Y and X should spend time with the mother. The Family Report supports a strong loving relationship between them. Of concern is whether the mother is able to regulate herself emotionally to meet the children’s needs, the current state of her mental health, whether or not she has or is currently engaging in illicit drug use or abuse of prescription medication, and the stability of her accommodation to support unsupervised time.
The evidence from the supervision reports and Family Report support a finding that both children are attuned to their mother’s emotional needs. Both have been observed to comfort their mother when she has been distressed. It would appear on the evidence that this is practised behaviour for them. The problem this gives rise to from the children’s perspective is that they see themselves as responsible for meeting their mother’s emotional needs when she should be meeting their needs. It also suggests a pattern of this behaviour has developed over time, particularly for X as the older child.
The mother has been diagnosed and treated with ADHD medication. She previously attended a psychiatrist, Dr J, and records of Dr J are before the Court. Dr J’s records support the mother being advised against breastfeeding whilst taking ADHD medication. In correspondence between Dr J and the general practitioner which has been tendered, it is clear this was a matter that was discussed between the mother and her psychiatrist. Different options were explored, as reported to the GP, with respect to alternate medication or whether the medication should continue, and she not breastfeed Y. The mother indicated that the medication usually prescribed to her was that which was most effective for meeting her needs for stability for her mental health. On that basis, she agreed she would follow advice not to breastfeed whilst on the medication. These matters are set out at Exhibit 5, and in particular correspondence dated 14 August 2023 and 3 November 2023.
In February 2024, the records show Dr J being contacted by the Child Protection Unit at H Hospital. The father had become concerned the mother was using substances whilst breastfeeding Y. He took Y for a urine test at H Hospital in January 2023. The test results showed the presence of Seroquel, amphetamine and caprolactam. Exhibit 8 of the exhibits before me is a request for a drug test from the mother which she did not comply with.
The mother saw Dr K psychiatrist, having sought a referral by Dr J for a female psychiatrist, in February 2024 and March 2024. I have no evidence Dr K has seen the mother since, nor that the mother has any regular contact with a treating psychiatrist. Dr K noted in correspondence her lack of concern about the mother taking ADHD medication whilst breastfeeding. It is not clear on the material from Dr K whether she was aware of the issues raised by H Hospital and the involvement of the Child Protection Unit. That two psychiatrists may have a difference of opinion about risks to infants from ADHD medication passing through breastmilk does not resolve the issue of the mother continuing to take the medication.
Dr J’s advice to the mother was clear. The mother indicated to Dr J she would comply with that advice. She did not. This is concerning, both in terms of her frankness with her treating psychiatrist, and her understanding of the risks to Y outlined by Dr J of her taking medication whilst breastfeeding. The mother has provided no explanation of the presence of Seroquel, an antipsychotic medication, in Y’s urine. The mother denied she took the medication and denied that she gave it to Y, or that Y could have consumed a tablet at any time in her care.
At the time of the urine testing in early 2024, Y was spending brief periods with her father and was otherwise with her mother, being breastfed. However it happened, the medication was consumed by Y. Clearly, this should not have occurred. This issue leaves a lingering concern about safety of Y, particularly given her age, in the unsupervised care of her mother.
I note that with respect to the AVO for the protection of the father and the children, there has been no issue of any breach of those orders by the mother over the 12-month currency of the order.
With respect to the mother’s reliability for the time arrangements, she has been late on a number of occasions for supervised visits. Although orders were made for time to occur weekly, it occurs fortnightly, as that is what can be provided by B Contact Centre. The mother missed one supervised visit without providing any notice to the centre on 19 August 2025. She did not reply to their calls and attempts to contact her. The extent to which this was unusual for the mother is clear from the contact centre’s staff’s decision to contact NSW Police. Centre staff were clearly extremely concerned by her lack of response, being atypical. There have been occasions otherwise where she has advised that she would not be available before the visit was due to start. Generally, looking at the contact records, the mother has been diligent with attending for time as ordered, or advising if she could not attend.
The mother has not utilised electronic communication with the children available to her through orders. She told me during submissions she has not found it helpful. This means, for the children, that they only see their mother each fortnight. It is unfortunate the mother has taken the approach of not pursuing electronic communication. It may have been useful for the children, even if brief, to see and hear their mother between supervised visits. I accept that she simply has not taken up that possibility.
The other issues with respect to concern about the children’s safety during time with their mother arise from issues with her use of a motor vehicle. She had a serious accident in late 2024 when she fell asleep at the wheel driving between Sydney and City L. I have in evidence toll records from the car used by the mother. That provides evidence of the frequency and timing of her driving between Sydney and City L. It is clear from those records that she has made that trip frequently, and at all times of the day and night. I accept, given the extensive records for the period prior to Y spending overnights in her father’s care, Y is likely to have been in the car with the mother making that trip on regular occasions and during times when one would expect she would be asleep in bed.
The records demonstrate issues with the mother’s driving, in particular with speeding infringements. It is unclear whether Y has been in the car with her mother at some of those times. There would be an obvious risk to her and, indeed, X’s safety if that were the case and she was driving over the speed limit. This is not an issue for the children whilst time between them and their mother is supervised.
In April 2025, the mother undertook a random drug test while driving. She was found to have drugs in her system and lost her licence. On 27 October 2025, the ICL requested the mother complete a drug test pursuant to the orders in place. The mother did not do so. These two incidents leave a concern as to the mother’s current use of drugs. Clearly, drug use whilst the children are in her care would be adverse to their interests, both in terms of their physical and emotional safety.
Dr M provided a psychological assessment on both parents. With respect to the mother, it is concerning that it does not appear she is currently engaged with a psychiatrist. The material from Dr J makes clear how important ongoing psychiatric support has been for her, both in terms of managing her ADHD and generally assisting her functioning. It is not known what the mother’s current living arrangements are, which also has an influence on her stability and mental health. The lack of evidence from the mother means those matters are not known. The last known was that she was living in Airbnb-style accommodation, which would clearly suggest a lack of stability.
The challenges of caring for a two-year-old and a five-year-old, as the children are now, are different from caring for two school-aged children. Although not self-sufficient, the children will not require the same level of diligence in care as they do now, particularly Y. I accept that supervision of the children’s time with their mother is necessary until Y commences school in 2028.
The orders require the mother to provide evidence of mental health treatment and clear drug tests for the year prior to unsupervised time commencing. These are, in my view, appropriate additional safety nets for the children. Apart from being indicia that the mother has achieved a degree of stability that she is able to attend to those matters, the two aspects, being ongoing mental health treatment and clear drug tests, support her being able to meet her own needs. It is less likely, for example, that she will be emotionally labile and put the children in the position of managing her needs.
The orders that are proposed and supported by the father and the ICL have the benefit for the children of providing for them a relationship with their mother that is safe. For those reasons, I will be making those orders with respect to supervised time.
With respect to the costs of supervision, the mother’s income is not known. She has been working in the sex industry. She pays no child support to the father, who has not sought payment through the Child Support Agency. His evidence is he did not wish to place that as an additional burden on the mother. The father is employed on a gross income of $170,000 per annum, inclusive of superannuation. He understands he may receive bonuses annually of between five to 10 per cent of his income, but he has no guarantee that will occur. He is living with his parents, who provide him with support for the children’s care.
He and his parents pool their resources, on his evidence. For example, he places his income into a mortgage offset account to provide a benefit with respect to the reduction of his parents’ mortgage. These proceedings have been expensive. I have before me the cost notice, which sets that out in no uncertain terms. The father has acquired debts to his parents and other friends and relatives to meet the costs of this litigation, and the costs of the children’s needs. His Financial Statement sets out the level of debt that he is currently meeting from his income. His evidence is it will take him five years to meet all the debts arising from the hearing, including the period he ceased work to settle them into his full-time care.
He has not provided any formal loan agreements in support of the debts. I have before me the cost notice. I do not doubt his evidence as to the financial toll of these proceedings. Litigation is expensive. The father has borne the totality of the costs and expenses for the children. He has ensured the children are engaged in appropriate activities, such as swimming lessons and dance. They have attended childcare and preschool. All those matters cost money.
His Financial Statement indicates he has $600 per week income in excess of expenses. The orders sought by the ICL seek the father meet one half of the expenses, with the mother meeting the other half for supervision.
The benefit for the children in spending time with their mother on a supervised basis, as it is presently, is clear. This is not disputed by the father. It is critically important for their emotional needs that they spend regular time with their mother, as referred to in the Family Report. I accept that.
B Contact Centre is a service that provides subsidised supervision, because they receive a degree of government funding, as I understand it. The costs of a session are between $40 to $80 on a sliding scale, in accordance with the parents’ income. I accept that the father has the capacity to meet half of those costs.
I have no evidence of the mother’s income, though I understand she is meeting half of those costs. It is likely that her income varies from time-to-time. It is not known whether the parties will be able to maintain a place at a supervised centre which is subsidised. A private organisation entails significant costs of approximately $280 to $330 per hour. There is usually a minimum of at least two hours, if not three hours, time to occur.
Over the next two years or longer, should the mother not meet the requirements with respect to drug testing and mental health assistance, it may be the parties will be required to meet private supervision costs. This adds to my concern that they share the expense to ensure that the time occurs. Imposing the costs of half of the supervision on the father is, in my view, necessary to ensure time arrangements continue. I accept that the father can afford to do so, although I acknowledge that this is an additional burden on him and will no doubt stretch his resources. He has the support of his parents, and I have already referred to their pooling resources.
In terms of the restraint on the mother’s brother, in circumstances where there is no evidence that the mother is currently engaging with psychiatric assistance, I am concerned about her ability to discern what may be safe situations for the children, including safe or otherwise with her brother. From the police records in evidence, he was charged with multiple offences in 2025. Those matters have not yet been finalised, it appears, on the evidence. He otherwise has a fairly extensive criminal record. The mother has previously left Y in his care at a time she was a very vulnerable infant. This gives rise to a concerning lack of judgment about risk from her brother.
I accept that it is necessary for the children’s safety that they not be brought into contact with Mr G. The mother made a submission that she currently has no contact with her brother, and she did not oppose the orders sought restraining the children from having contact with him. I note this is in contrast to her discussions with the report writer that she had a close relationship with her brother. It is for these reasons that I have made these findings to ensure that the children are not brought into contact with Mr G, whatever the mother’s view about that. This will protect the children into the future.
As regards long-term decision-making for the children, I accept the submission that there is no effective communication between the parents. I also accept that the father has not provided the mother with all information about the children’s health. X has recovered well from a potentially serious medical condition, and he is doing very well now. The evidence supports that the father has competently managed the health needs for both children, including pursuing urinalysis testing of Y when he was concerned for her.
It is important the mother has access to relevant material about the children and their health needs, educational needs and the like. That is so whether or not some action is required as a consequence of the information. The father has taken the view that, the mother did not need to be informed about the results of testing if it was not serious, or nothing was required. This was the case when Y’s hearing was tested. It is important the mother is across all information, particularly regarding medical matters for the children. The father accepted in his evidence that he has not been as diligent as he should be, and that he proposes providing such material to the mother in the future. The orders will require it.
The mother needs to access information, and I note that school and childcare information is generally available directly from the source. It will be helpful for the mother and her ongoing relationship with the children to engage with those service providers, so she understands the progress of the children through their education, extracurricular activities and other matters. It is for these reasons that I am making the orders sought by the father and supported by the ICL, with the order for costs of supervised time as sought by the ICL.
In terms of the ICL’s costs, counsel for the ICL indicated he was obliged by Legal Aid to make an application for costs. He relied on the cost notice tendered. The ICL made the application whilst acknowledging the difficulty for each of the parents in meeting even the modest costs sought. As referred to above, I have no evidence of the mother’s income. I have evidence of the father’s level of debt, and that he is meeting the totality of the children’s expenses. Both parents will have an ongoing responsibility for meeting half of the costs of supervision.
In these circumstances, I do not propose making an order for the parties to contribute to the costs of the ICL. I am concerned that to do so would directly disadvantage the children in terms of the parents’ capacity to meet half of the costs of supervision and the father meeting all of their other expenses.
